text: Hello friends, this is a case of laparoscopic mini gastric bypass. This is a forty-seven year old woman with BMI forty-five. She has already undergone laparoscopic polycystectomy before and now we are performing the MGB, mini gastric bypass.

The surgeon prepares to insert a varus needle at the inferior crease of the umbilicus, followed by the placement of a supraumbilical port for the telescope. Additional ports are positioned at the left epigastric region for the stapler, left and right hypochondrium, and left anterior axillary line, establishing the standard port configuration for the procedure.

The surgeon uses a 45-liter insufflator in bariatric mode to inflate the abdominal cavity, reaching a pressure of 15 mmHg. The supraumbilical port is utilized for the insertion of the telescope.
The surgeon introduces a 10mm port for the telescope, allowing for inspection of the abdominal cavity. The insufflator tubing is attached, and the flow rate is increased to 20 liters per minute.

The surgeon observes minor adhesions of the omentum near the gallbladder area in the right hypochondrium. The right hypochondriac port is used for the stapler, while the left hypochondriac port is positioned for the surgeon's right hand to operate with ligature or other instruments.

The surgeon utilizes the epigastric port to lift the omentum, while the right hypochondriac port accommodates a lever retractor. The left hypochondriac port is manipulated with the right hand using a ligature instrument to hold structures in place. A lever instrument is used for lifting, but not the Nathanson lever retractor.
The surgeon lifts the lesser omentum at the level of the second gastric pedicle and creates an omental window. The window is then enlarged to inspect for any adhesions below the stomach.

The surgeon uses a ligature to create a window in the lesser omentum, revealing minimal adhesions. Through the epigastric port, a stapler is used to create a horizontal line for forming the gastric tube. The first cartridge is fired, and the gastric tube is formed, with the busy being introduced into the tube.

The surgeon begins taking the vertical staple line in the retrogastric area, ensuring there are no adhesions present. Any adhesions encountered are separated using either a harmonic scalpel or ligature. The vertical staple line is progressively fired, with inspection and adhesiolysis performed before continuing.

The surgeon carefully fires the second staple, then uses a grasper to lift the stomach, providing a clear view of the retrogastric area. The visual inspection confirms minimal adhesion in the retrogastric space.

The surgeon introduces the third staple cartridge to continue the gastric bypass procedure. The retrogastric area is inspected, revealing minor adhesions, which are addressed using a harmonic device for adhesiolysis.

The surgeon introduces the third staple cartridge near the gastric bushy to create the gastric tube, emphasizing the restrictive component of the mini gastric bypass. The staple is fired after a brief pause to ensure precision.

The surgeon uses a grasper to lift the stomach, allowing inspection of the posterior gastric area for adhesions. The telescope is inserted into the retro gastric area to ensure a clear view, confirming minimal adhesion before proceeding with staple firing.

The surgeon uses a ligature to perform adhesiolysis, carefully separating adhesions between the spleen and the anterior stomach, as well as the retrograstric fat cushion. Blunt dissection is performed to create a window, allowing access up to the diaphragm. Mobilization of the peritoneum over the left crus is also undertaken to facilitate this approach.

The surgeon carefully lifts the stomach with the left hand while using the right hand to guide the telescope into the retrogastric tunnel. The ligature is used cautiously to avoid collateral damage or burning the stomach. As the blunt dissection progresses, the shiny fascia of the left crus and the diaphragm become visible, indicating the creation of a window beyond the gastric fundus.

The surgeon creates a window at the diaphragm and applies suction before introducing a 60 mm staple cartridge. The staple is fired, cutting through the tissue, and the procedure reaches the fundus. A small portion of the fundus tag remains attached at the gastroesophageal junction, visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon removes the previous cartridge and introduces a new one to cut a small portion of tissue. After firing the stapler, suction irrigation is performed, confirming no bleeding. The surgeon then lifts the omentum and moves the small bowel to reach the first part of the jejunum near the ligament of Treitz, noting the presence of significant fat in the morbidly obese patient.

The surgeon tilts the tablet to pull the intestinal loop, reaching the ligament of Treitz. The first part of the jejunum is identified, and the transverse colon and greater omentum are lifted to ensure the ligament of Treitz is clearly visible.
The surgeon measures and walks over the jejunum, ensuring there is no torsion, while counting the length in increments, keeping the jejunum oriented towards the left side.

The surgeon measures and pulls the jejunum to reach a length of 2.5 meters, preparing for the single anastomosis required in the mini gastric bypass procedure. Care is taken to avoid accidental rotation of the intestine during this process.
The surgeon creates a window in the stomach just above the staple line on the anterior wall. The harmonic device is inserted into the perforated stomach, with the option to use a hook for this step.
The surgeon inserts the gastric calibration tube into the stomach and positions it inside. The assistant pulls the tube, ensuring no subserosal pocket forms during the process.

The lumen of the jejunum is visible as the surgeon cuts and perforates this section. Graspers are introduced to manipulate the tissue, followed by the insertion of a stapler. One jaw of the stapler is positioned in the jejunum, while the other jaw is aligned with the stomach to perform a side-to-side anastomosis using a 60mm cartridge. Both ends are fully pulled to ensure proper alignment.
The surgeon ensures both edges of the tissue are aligned evenly without compromising blood supply, holding them correctly before firing the stapler to create an anastomosis.

The surgeon removes the instrument from the surgical site, followed by the application of suction to lift the tissue. The buzi is visible, and the lumen of the jejunum is clearly exposed, confirming a successful anastomosis.
The surgeon begins closing the suture line using a barbed suture, applying a two-layer closure technique to secure the staple perforation site.
The surgeon approximates the serosal layer using a barb suture, which features laser cuts every millimeter for self-holding. Despite this property, the surgeon ties a knot to secure the suture.

The surgeon begins tying a surgeon's knot with a double wrap, followed by two alternating wraps. The knotting sequence includes a C, reverse C, and another C pattern, completing the two-one-one configuration of the surgeon's knot.
The surgeon uses a starter knot to begin closing the staple hole between the jejunum and the stomach line, gradually securing the tissue with precise suturing.

The surgeon uses a continuous barb suture made of polygalactane to perform edge-to-edge suturing. The left instrument gently lifts the falciform ligament while the surgeon takes bites along the suture line.

The surgeon performs continuous suturing along the posterior aspect of the anastomosis, ensuring the corners are adequately sutured to prevent leaks. The mucosa is inverted to avoid occlusion of the lumen during this single anastomosis procedure.

The surgeon uses the left hand to support the suture while the right hand takes the bite, ensuring the sutures are placed closely without excessive pulling to prevent cut-through during the gastric bypass procedure.
The surgeon reaches the corners of the anastomosis and interlocks the sutures, ensuring closure extends to the healthy margin. The corners are carefully closed with locking stitches to prevent leaks.
The surgeon interlocks the suture at the current point and continues the suturing process, ensuring to extend the suture to the healthy margin of the tissue.
The surgeon interlocks the suture at the healthy margin of the gastric tissue, ensuring secure closure by maintaining tension on the suture line.
The surgeon lifts the tissue to inspect the sutured margin, taking an additional bite on the margin. The suture is then used to perform a second layer of serosa-to-serosa suturing.

The surgeon uses a blue dye to check for leaks, ensuring that the corners of the sutured area are embedded and buried to prevent any leakage.
The surgeon carefully continues suturing to maintain the integrity of the seromuscular layer, successfully closing the entire staple hole.

The surgeon uses a barb suture to terminate the suture line, performing a double wrap followed by a single wrap to tie a knot. Once the suture is closed, scissors are used to remove the excess suture material, and the lumen is checked for integrity.

The surgeon uses scissors to cut the suture, then grasps the suture and its tail to pull it out. Two graspers are used to hold either side of the lumen, ensuring stability and exposure for further inspection.

The surgeon uses a long jaw clasp to occlude the area while the anesthetist introduces methylene blue dye, resulting in visible distension without any leakage. The dye spreads evenly on either side, confirming the integrity of the stapler line and the stomach, including the gastroesophageal junction, with no blue color indicating leaks.

The surgeon checks for leaks in the surgical site, confirming there is no leakage. A suture is placed on the serosa, and both the efferent and afferent limbs are fixed. The afferent limb is anchored to the stomach part, aligning parallel to the gastric tube and reducing the load on the staple line.

The surgeon uses a white reel to fix the anchoring suture, ensuring it is tension-free and securely tightened. The suture is pulled and tightened carefully to avoid tearing, followed by the application of a C and reverse C knot twice, using a surgeon's knot.
The surgeon ties a single knot to minimize tension on the staple line, then cuts the suture. Another suture is prepared for fixation.
The surgeon uses two graspers to hold either side of the lumen and some part of the stomach, preparing to fix the afferent loop to the stomach with a suture and tie a knot.
The surgeon tightens a knot using the C and reverse C technique to release tension on the jejunum over the staple line. The omentum is gently pulled into view.

The surgeon completes the final C-shaped suture and pulls the omentum to cover the entire anastomotic line of the gastric bypass. The omentum is fixed to the stomach using a titanium clip or a knot, ensuring coverage of the single bypass anastomosis.
This was just a simple case of mini gastric bypass where we tried to explain everything. Thank you very much. God bless you.
